metadata.seed: I would like to work on my avoidance behavior. I actually have an opportunity which I was looking for from a very long time to give a presentation. And now that I have this opportunity in front of me, I'm trying to avoid it.

patient.query: I would like to work on my avoidance behavior. I actually have an opportunity which I was looking for from a very long time to give a presentation. And now that I have this opportunity in front of me, I'm trying to avoid it.

automatic_thoughts: I want to avoid this
I'm not ready for this

llm_response.response: What thoughts pop up for you when you imagine stepping onto the stage? How do you know whether those thoughts are accurate or just a fear of the unknown? What might happen if you decided to give the presentation, even if it feels uncomfortable?

patient.query:
When I think about stepping onto the stage, I feel this tight knot in my stomach. It’s like a rush of thoughts telling me I’m going to mess up or everyone will just see how unqualified I really am. Sometimes I wonder if those thoughts are just my nerves, but it’s hard to shake off that feeling of dread. 

If I did go ahead and give the presentation, I think I'd feel really exposed. Part of me worries that I’d just freeze up and forget what I

triggers: thinking about stepping onto the stage
automatic_thoughts: I'm going to mess up
everyone will see how unqualified I really am
I'd just freeze up and forget what I prepared

llm_response.response: It sounds like the thought of stepping onto the stage brings a tight knot in your stomach and a sense of being exposed. What evidence do you have that you will mess up or that people will see you as unqualified? Can you recall any moments when you’ve handled a similar situation and felt more confident?

patient.query:
I guess I don’t really have solid evidence—more like a gut feeling. It’s just that I’ve felt that way before, like in school when I’d get called on in class and just stumble over my words. I can recall a couple of times I did okay, but they feel so far away now, like I’m not the same person who did those things. 

Honestly, it’s easier to focus on the times when I felt like I failed. Those moments seem to stick with

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have solid evidence
I’ll stumble over my words
I’m not the same person who did those things
I focus on the times I felt like I failed

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding onto a strong sense that you’ve failed in the past. What evidence do you have that supports this feeling, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? How might you view those past successes if you were talking to a supportive friend?

patient.query:
Yeah, I guess it does feel like I’m clinging to those failures. They just seem so vivid in my mind. I think about the times when I forgot my lines or got laughed at, and it’s hard to see past those moments. 

If I were talking to a friend, I would probably tell them that everyone has off days and that it doesn’t define their worth. But when I’m in my own head, it’s like I can’t give myself that same kindness. It

automatic_thoughts: I’m clinging to those failures
I can’t see past those moments
I can’t give myself kindness

llm_response.response: It sounds like those moments feel like a defining part of you, but they’re just one piece of a larger story. What evidence do you have that you’re not capable of treating yourself with the same kindness you would offer a friend? Have you noticed any times when you’ve been gentle with yourself, even if it was brief?

patient.query:
I guess I haven’t really stopped to think about it that way. I mean, there are moments—like when I tried to celebrate small wins at work recently. I didn’t dwell on the mistakes I made, and that felt kind of nice. But then there are other times, when I just beat myself up afterward for not doing better. 

It’s hard to reconcile those two sides of me. One minute I can be proud, and the next I’m back in that critical headspace.

automatic_thoughts: I beat myself up for not doing better
I can be proud
I’m back in that critical headspace

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught between moments of pride and moments of harsh self‑criticism, which can feel confusing and even shame‑laden. That tug‑of‑war is a common experience, especially when we’re trying to celebrate progress while still holding ourselves to high standards. It’s understandable that these feelings feel hard to reconcile.

patient.query:
Yeah, it is confusing. I feel like I should be able to just choose to focus on the good moments, but it’s like those negative thoughts just have this grip on me. It’s frustrating, you know? I want to believe in myself and acknowledge what I’ve done, but then that nagging voice pops up and tells me it’s not enough. 

I guess part of me feels like if I don’t strive for more, then I’m not living up to my potential. But

automatic_thoughts: I should be able to just choose to focus on the good moments
Those negative thoughts have a grip on me
That nagging voice tells me it’s not enough
If I don’t strive for more, then I’m not living up to my potential

llm_response.response: I hear how frustrating it feels when those negative thoughts seem to take over. What evidence do you have that the voice telling you “it’s not enough” is accurate? How might you imagine a different way of looking at the same moments that could feel more balanced?

patient.query:
It’s hard to pinpoint exact evidence, really. I think it’s just a feeling that I’ve carried for a long time, like I’ve set these expectations for myself that can sometimes feel impossible. When I think about the times I’ve succeeded, I can’t help but feel like it was just luck or that others could’ve done it better. 

Imagining a different perspective sounds nice, but I often wonder if that’s just me trying to cover up the truth. I mean, could it

automatic_thoughts: I’ve set these expectations for myself that can sometimes feel impossible
It was just luck or that others could’ve done it better
Imagining a different perspective sounds nice
That’s just me trying to cover up the truth

llm_response.response: What makes you feel that your expectations are impossible? How might you look at the times you succeeded to see what actually contributed to those outcomes? What would a close friend say if they heard you describe those successes?

patient.query:
It’s hard to explain, but I think my expectations come from this idea that I always have to be the best or at least really great at everything I do. It feels like if I’m not perfect, then I’ve failed. And looking back at the successes, it’s usually overshadowed by the anxiety of what I didn’t do right. 

If a close friend heard me talk about those moments, I guess they’d probably remind me to recognize the hard work I put in, or they

automatic_thoughts: I always have to be the best
If I'm not perfect, then I've failed
My successes are overshadowed by anxiety

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy expectation that everything you do must be flawless, and when it isn’t, you feel like a failure. That can make even your successes feel dimmed by lingering worries. It’s understandable that a friend might point out the effort you’ve put in. How does that idea of being a hard worker feel to you when you think about it?

patient.query:
It’s a bit of a mixed bag, honestly. On one hand, I do feel proud of the effort I put in because I know I try hard. But then I get this nagging feeling, like it still isn’t enough or that I should’ve done more. It’s almost like I’m waiting for someone to notice the hard work, but it feels like if they don’t, then it doesn’t count.

Sometimes I wonder if it means anything if I don’t get that validation,

automatic_thoughts: it still isn’t enough
I should’ve done more
if they don’t notice, it doesn’t count
wondering if it means anything

entailment: Character trait finding of effort at work (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re proud of your effort, yet you’re also feeling that it might not be enough unless someone else notices. What kinds of situations make you feel that the lack of external acknowledgment diminishes your accomplishment? And how might you view your effort if you decided that your own sense of worth doesn’t depend on others’ reactions?

patient.query:
It’s a bit of a mixed bag, honestly. On one hand, I do feel proud of the effort I put in because I know I try hard. But then I get this nagging feeling, like it still isn’t enough or that I should’ve done more. It’s almost like I’m waiting for someone to notice the hard work, but it feels like if they don’t, then it doesn’t count.

Sometimes I wonder if it means anything if I don’t get that validation,

automatic_thoughts: it still isn’t enough
I should’ve done more
if they don’t notice, it doesn’t count
wonder if it means anything

entailment: Character trait finding of effort at work (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re proud of your effort, yet you’re also feeling that it might not be enough if others don’t notice. What evidence do you have that the lack of external validation means your effort isn’t worthwhile? How might you gauge the value of your work from your own perspective instead?
